title: What the Cache Forgets: LRU & Eviction in SWR/React Query
description: You reach for TanStack Query and SWR every day — but do you know *when* they evict, and why a hand-rolled memo `Map` quietly leaks memory? Trace LRU eviction (hash map + doubly linked list), contrast LFU and TTL, reason about cache hits and misses, and cap a memoization `Map` at 100 entries. Anchored in 'why did React Query refetch this?'

questions.title: Why did React Query refetch this?
questions.description: A component reads `useQuery({ queryKey: ['user', 7], queryFn })` with TanStack Query's **default** options. On mount you instantly see the previously cached user, then a network request fires and the data refreshes. The query key never changed.

options.text: Default `staleTime` is `0`, so the cached data was already stale; mounting a fresh observer triggered a background refetch (`refetchOnMount`) while the stale data was shown immediately.
A built-in LRU size cap evicted the entry, so the data had to be fetched from scratch.
The `gcTime` timer elapsed and garbage-collected the cache entry, forcing a reload.
Reading the query created a brand-new cache entry under a different key.

questions.explanation:
TanStack Query is *stale-while-revalidate*: it serves whatever is cached **instantly**, then decides whether to refresh in the background. Two defaults make the refetch happen here:

- `staleTime: 0` — data is considered stale the moment it arrives, so it's always a refetch candidate.
- `refetchOnMount: true` — mounting a new observer for a stale query kicks off a background refetch (the same is true for `refetchOnWindowFocus` and `refetchOnReconnect`).

Crucially, you still *saw* the cached data — nothing was evicted. The LRU and `gcTime` options describe **removal** from the cache, but neither happened: the entry was present (you rendered it) and active (a component was mounted, so the `gcTime` timer wasn't even running). To stop the refetch, raise `staleTime` (e.g. `staleTime: 60_000`). SWR works the same way: `useSWR` returns cached data and revalidates on focus/reconnect by default.

questions.title: What correctly distinguishes `staleTime` from `gcTime`?
questions.description: In TanStack Query v5, `gcTime` is the option formerly called `cacheTime`.

options.text: `staleTime` is how long data stays *fresh* (no background refetch); `gcTime` is how long an **inactive** query — one with no mounted observers — is kept in the cache before it's removed.
They are two names for the same option; `gcTime` is simply the v5 rename of `staleTime`.
`staleTime` deletes the entry from the cache, while `gcTime` keeps a stale copy around.
`gcTime` must always be set smaller than `staleTime`, or the cache throws an error.

questions.explanation:
These two timers answer different questions:

- **`staleTime`** (default `0`): *How long is this data trusted as fresh?* While fresh, no background refetch fires on mount/focus/reconnect. It never removes anything.
- **`gcTime`** (default `5 * 60 * 1000` = 5 minutes): *Once nobody is using this query, how long before we free its memory?* The garbage-collection timer only starts when the query goes **inactive** (its last observer unmounts). If the query becomes active again before the timer fires, the cached data is reused.

`gcTime` is the v5 rename of `cacheTime` — **not** of `staleTime`. And there's no rule forcing `gcTime < staleTime`; in practice you usually want `gcTime` to outlast `staleTime`, so a re-mounted query can still find its (possibly stale) data and revalidate instead of starting cold.

questions.title: Which key does the LRU cache evict when `put('D', 4)` runs?
questions.explanation:
Track the recency order as `[least-recently-used … most-recently-used]`:

```text
put('A',1) -> [A]
put('B',2) -> [A, B]
put('C',3) -> [A, B, C]
get('A')   -> [B, C, A]   // A touched -> moved to the MRU end
put('D',4) -> over capacity -> evict LRU 'B' -> [C, A, D]
```

The trap is picking `A` because it was inserted first. But LRU evicts by **last use**, not insertion order: `get('A')` promoted `A` to the most-recently-used end, so `B` — untouched since its insertion — became the least-recently-used and was evicted. After the insert the order is `[C, A, D]`, so `C` is next in line, not `A`.

questions.title: Why does an LRU cache's list need to be **doubly** linked?
questions.description: The textbook O(1) LRU cache pairs a hash map (key → node) with a linked list that orders nodes by recency.

options.text: Removing or moving an arbitrary node in O(1) requires splicing out both neighbor links; in a singly linked list you can't reach a node's predecessor without an O(n) scan.
A doubly linked list lets you binary-search the keys in O(log n).
Two pointers per node are needed so each entry can store both its key and its value.
Singly linked lists can't be traversed in order, so iteration would be impossible.

questions.explanation:
Every `get`/`put` hit has to **move a node to the most-recently-used end**, and every eviction has to **unlink the least-recently-used node**. Both are arbitrary-position removals. To unlink a node in O(1) you must rewire its previous and next neighbors:

```text
prev <-> node <-> next   becomes   prev <-> next
```

With a *singly* linked list you only have `next` pointers, so finding the predecessor of a given node means walking from the head — O(n) — which destroys the whole point of an O(1) cache. The hash map already gives O(1) key→node lookup; the doubly linked list gives O(1) reordering. Searching is never done by walking the list (that's the map's job), so the 'binary search' and 'can't traverse' options are red herrings.

questions.title: What happens to this memo `Map` over a long-running server's lifetime?

options.text: The `Map` grows without bound — every id, its cached value, and anything that value references is retained for the life of the process. It's a memory leak.
Nothing leaks: once a `reqId` goes out of scope elsewhere, the garbage collector drops the matching `Map` entry automatically.
V8 silently caps a `Map` at 1024 entries, so memory plateaus.
Because keys are unique, each call overwrites the previous entry and memory stays roughly constant.

questions.explanation:
A `Map` holds **strong references** to its keys and values, so an entry lives until you explicitly `delete` it (or drop the whole `Map`). Keying by an ever-growing set of unique ids means the cache only grows — the textbook unbounded-cache memory leak. The GC can't help: the `Map` itself is reachable, so everything it points at is reachable too.

Two real fixes:

- **Bound the cache** with an eviction policy (LRU/LFU/TTL) so old entries are removed.
- If you key by **object identity** and only want an entry to live as long as its key object does, use a `WeakMap`, whose entries become GC-eligible once the key is otherwise unreachable. (A `WeakMap` can't take primitive keys like a numeric id and isn't iterable, so it doesn't help when you need a size cap.)

questions.title: Which statements correctly describe a hash map + doubly linked list LRU cache?

options.text: On a cache **hit**, `get` moves the accessed node to the most-recently-used end so it isn't evicted next.
The hash map stores key → node references, giving O(1) lookup without scanning the list.
When `put` exceeds capacity, the node at the least-recently-used end is unlinked **and** its key is deleted from the map.
Nodes are kept in sorted key order so the cache can answer range queries.

questions.explanation:
The hash map and the list divide the labor: the **map** answers *'where is this key's node?'* in O(1), and the **doubly linked list** answers *'what's the recency order?'* and supports O(1) splicing.

- Hit → promote the node to the MRU end (otherwise recency information goes stale).
- Lookup → O(1) via the map; you never walk the list to find a key.
- Overflow → drop the LRU-end node **and** delete its key from the map. Forgetting the map deletion is the classic bug: it leaks memory and desynchronizes `size` from the list.

The list is ordered by **recency**, not by key value, so it can't answer range/sorted queries — that would need a different structure such as a balanced BST.

questions.title: Which statements about LRU, LFU, and TTL eviction are accurate?

options.text: LRU evicts the entry that has gone the longest without being accessed.
LFU evicts the entry with the fewest accesses — so a briefly viral entry can linger long after it stops being useful, because raw counts don't age.
TTL evicts an entry once its age exceeds a fixed lifetime, regardless of how recently or often it was used.
A pure TTL policy guarantees the cache never holds more than a fixed number of entries.

questions.explanation:
Each policy optimizes for a different signal:

- **LRU** — recency. Evicts the least-recently-*used* entry. Great when access has temporal locality.
- **LFU** — frequency. Evicts the least-frequently-*used* entry. Its classic weakness is **cache pollution**: an item that once spiked in popularity keeps a high count and resists eviction forever, unless you add aging/decay (e.g. windowed counts).
- **TTL** — age. Evicts on a clock, independent of usage. Ideal for data with a known freshness window.

The false statement confuses *age* with *count*. TTL bounds how **old** an entry can be, not how **many** exist: if 10,000 distinct keys arrive within the TTL window, all 10,000 live in the cache at once. To bound size you need a count-based policy like LRU/LFU (often combined with TTL).

questions.title: What makes TanStack Query refetch a stale query?
questions.description: Assume **default** options and a query that is currently stale. Select all that apply.

options.text: A component mounts and subscribes as a new observer of the query (`refetchOnMount`).
The browser tab/window regains focus (`refetchOnWindowFocus`).
The network reconnects after the device was offline (`refetchOnReconnect`).
Another component simply reads the already-cached value, with no mount, focus, or reconnect event.

questions.explanation:
TanStack Query's automatic refetch switches are all *true* by default, and each only fires when the query is **stale** (which, with `staleTime: 0`, is immediately):

- `refetchOnMount` — a new observer mounts.
- `refetchOnWindowFocus` — the user returns to the tab.
- `refetchOnReconnect` — the network comes back.

What does **not** trigger a fetch is merely reading cached data. Selecting an existing entry returns it synchronously; without a mount/focus/reconnect event (or an explicit `refetch()`, `invalidateQueries`, or a `refetchInterval`), no request is made. SWR exposes the same switches under different names — `revalidateOnFocus` and `revalidateOnReconnect`.

questions.title: Which statements correctly describe how a plain JS `Map` behaves?
questions.description: You want a bounded, roughly-LRU cache capped at 100 entries, built on a plain JS `Map`. Select **all** that apply.

options.text: `cache.keys().next().value` returns the **oldest-inserted** key, a handy eviction target since `Map` iterates in insertion order.
Re-assigning an existing key with `cache.set(key, v)` updates the value but does **not** move the key to the end of the iteration order.
To approximate true LRU, on each hit you `delete` the key and `set` it again, moving it to the most-recently-used end.
`cache.set` automatically discards the oldest entry once `size` reaches 100, so no manual eviction is needed.

questions.title: After how many minutes is an inactive query garbage-collected?
questions.description: TanStack Query v5 with default settings; an **inactive** query is one with no mounted observers. Enter the number of minutes.
questions.explanation: The default `gcTime` is `5 * 60 * 1000` ms = **5 minutes**. The timer starts only when a query becomes *inactive* — i.e. its last observer (component) unmounts. If nothing re-subscribes within those 5 minutes, the entry is removed and its memory freed; re-mount before then and the cached data is reused (and revalidated if stale). This is unrelated to `staleTime` (default `0`), which governs *freshness*, not removal. `gcTime` was called `cacheTime` before v5.
